name: Shara
description: Shara was a minor Sumerian deity, primarily associated with agriculture and vegetation, particularly the life-giving power of irrigation and fertility of the land. He was worshipped primarily in the city of Umma, where he had a significant temple dedicated to him. While not as prominent as major deities like Enlil or Inanna, Shara played a crucial role in the daily lives of the Sumerians, who depended heavily on successful harvests for their survival. He was considered a protector of the fields and orchards, ensuring their abundance and fruitfulness. Shara's power was believed to stem from his connection to the earth and the divine forces that governed the natural world. He was often invoked in prayers and rituals aimed at promoting agricultural prosperity. Although specific myths about Shara are scarce, his importance is evident in the offerings made to him and the dedication of his temple in Umma, reflecting the deep connection between the Sumerians and the land they cultivated. His influence extended to the well-being of the community, ensuring food security and prosperity. Shara represented the life cycle of plants, from germination to harvest, embodying the cyclical nature of life and the importance of agriculture in Sumerian society. He was considered a vital link between the divine and the earthly realms, facilitating the flow of blessings and sustenance to the people.
subtitle: God of Agriculture and Vegetation

keyMyths.title: Shara's Blessing
keyMyths.description: Farmers prayed to Shara for a bountiful harvest. They offered him grain and other agricultural products in hopes of securing his favor and ensuring a successful yield.

keyMyths.title: The Temple of Shara in Umma
keyMyths.description: The temple dedicated to Shara in Umma was a center of agricultural rituals. Priests performed ceremonies to honor him and ensure the continued fertility of the land.

keyMyths.title: Shara and the Waters
keyMyths.description: Shara was associated with the life-giving waters used for irrigation. He was believed to control the flow of these waters, ensuring that the crops received adequate nourishment.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
sources.work: The Sumerians: Their History, Culture, and Character
sources.description: Provides general context on Sumerian religion and agriculture.
sources.text: Provides general context on Sumerian religion and agriculture.

sources.author: Thorkild Jacobsen

sources.text: Offers insights into the role of minor deities in Sumerian religious practices.
